fixes.napkin_math: **1. Centralized TCO (Monthly):** Data upload: 100,000 vehicles x 1 GB/vehicle = 100,000 GB = 100 TB. Cellular cost at $0.10/GB: 100,000 GB x $0.10 = **$10,000/month** (or at $10/GB enterprise cellular: **$1,000,000/month**). Cloud storage + compute: ~$5,000/month. **Total: ~$1,005,000/month.** | **2. Federated Learning TCO (Monthly):** Model update size (down + up): ~30 MB/vehicle/round. Rounds per month: 4. Active vehicles per round: 1,000 (1% sampling). Data transferred: 1,000 x 30 MB x 4 = 120 GB/month. Cellular cost at $10/GB: 120 x $10 = $1,200. FL server compute: ~$3,000/month. **Total: ~$4,200/month.** | **3. Verdict:** Centralized is ~240x more expensive. Cellular data cost dominates at 99.5% of centralized TCO. FL is the only viable option at fleet scale.

1. **Data per vehicle per round:** 25 MB (download) + 5 MB (upload) = 30 MB = 0.03 GB. | 2. **Vehicles sampled per round:** 1,000,000 x 5% = 50,000 (assume 5% participation). | 3. **Rounds per month:** 4. | 4. **Total data per month:** 50,000 x 0.03 GB x 4 = 6,000 GB. | 5. **Cost per GB (cellular):** $0.10. | 6. **Total monthly cost:** 6,000 x $0.10 = $600/month — well within budget. | But at $8/GB (enterprise cellular plan): 6,000 x $8 = **$48,000/month** — exceeds the $30,000 budget by 60%. | 7. **To meet budget at $8/GB:** Max data = $30,000 / $8 = 3,750 GB. Required reduction: cut participation to ~31,250 vehicles/round, or reduce model size, or compress updates to ~19 MB/vehicle.

**Plan A (Centralized Telemetry):** Each vehicle uploads 500 MB of drowsiness event clips per month. Fleet: 500,000 vehicles x 500 MB = 250 TB/month. Cellular cost at $0.10/GB: 250,000 GB x $0.10 = $25,000/month. Annual: **$300,000/year.** Cloud compute for retraining: ~$5,000/month = $60,000/year. **Plan A total: ~$360,000/year.** | **Plan B (Federated Learning):** Model diff per vehicle per round: 5 MB. Participation rate: 1% (5,000 vehicles/round). Rounds per month: 4. Monthly data: 5,000 x 5 MB x 4 = 100 GB. Cellular at $0.10/GB: $10/month. FL server: $2,000/month = $24,000/year. **Plan B total: ~$24,120/year.** | **Verdict:** Plan A is ~15x more expensive. At higher cellular rates ($10/GB), Plan A would cost $30M/year — completely infeasible. FL wins overwhelmingly on cost and provides privacy as a bonus.

**Centralized:** 10K vehicles x 5 clips/mo x 12 months = 600K clips/yr. Each clip ~300 MB raw video. Upload volume: 600K x 0.3 GB = 180,000 GB. Upload cost at $0.02/GB: 180,000 x $0.02 = $3,600. Labeling: 600K clips x $5/clip = $3,000,000. **Centralized total: ~$3.0M/yr.** | **Federated:** 1,000 vehicles/week x 52 weeks = 52,000 round-participations/yr. Model update size: 50 MB each. Data volume: 52,000 x 0.05 GB = 2,600 GB. Cellular at $10/GB: 2,600 x $10 = $26,000. FL servers: $50,000/yr. **FL total: ~$76,000/yr.** | **Verdict:** FL is ~39x cheaper. Labeling dominates centralized TCO at 99.9%.
fixes.realistic_solution: The correct approach is to calculate the full TCO for each option, including all associated costs. The centralized approach has a hidden cost bomb: human data labeling at $5/clip for 600K clips/year is $3M — dwarfing the cheap cloud ingress. The FL approach eliminates labeling entirely by learning directly from on-device behavior, bringing the total to ~$76K/year. The per-GB cellular cost for FL ($10/GB) is 500x higher than cloud ingress ($0.02/GB), but the total data volume is so much smaller (2,600 GB vs 180,000 GB) that FL still wins by 39x.

fixes.napkin_math: **1. Centralized (Monthly data cost):** Each vehicle uploads 200 MB of anonymized video/telemetry per month. Fleet: 500,000 x 200 MB = 100 TB/month. Cellular at $0.10/GB: 100,000 GB x $0.10 = $10,000/month. Cloud storage + compute: $5,000/month. **Monthly OpEx: $15,000.** 3-year total: 36 x $15,000 = **$540,000.** | **2. Federated (One-time + recurring):** On-device compute module CapEx: 500,000 x $2 amortized = $1,000,000 (one-time). FL server infra: $3,000/month x 36 = $108,000. Model update data: negligible (~100 GB/month at $0.10/GB = $10/month). **3-year total: ~$1,108,000.** | **3. Verdict:** Centralized ($540K) is cheaper in pure TCO over 3 years. However, FL's cost is front-loaded (CapEx), while centralized has ongoing regulatory risk. A single GDPR-scale breach fine ($20M+) makes FL the risk-adjusted winner.

**1. Given layer profile:** FLOPs = 500 MFLOPs (0.5 GFLOPs). Memory traffic (weights + activations) = 50 MB. | **2. Arithmetic Intensity (AI):** AI = FLOPs / Bytes = 500M / 50M = **10 Ops/Byte.** | **3. Orin ridge point:** Ridge = Peak Compute / Peak BW = 275 TOPS / 204.8 GB/s = **1,342 Ops/Byte.** | **4. Diagnosis:** AI (10) << Ridge (1,342), so this layer is deeply **memory-bandwidth bound.** The compute units are idle ~99% of the time waiting for data. | **5. Attainable performance:** BW-limited throughput = 204.8 GB/s x 10 Ops/Byte = 2.048 TOPS — only 0.7% of peak 275 TOPS. | **6. Layer latency:** 500M Ops / 2.048T Ops/s = **0.24 ms.** | **7. Fix:** Fuse this depthwise conv with adjacent pointwise conv to keep activations in SRAM, increasing effective AI. Pruning or quantizing further will NOT help — the bottleneck is memory, not compute.

**1. Sequence length (N):** 640x640 image with 16x16 patches: N = (640/16)^2 = 40^2 = **1,600 tokens.** | **2. Self-attention FLOPs (per head, per layer):** Q*K^T: 2 x N x N x d = 2 x 1600 x 1600 x 64 = 327M FLOPs. Attn*V: 2 x N x N x d = 327M FLOPs. Total per head: ~654M FLOPs. With 12 heads: ~7.8 GFLOPs. | **3. Memory traffic:** Q, K, V matrices: 3 x N x d x 12 heads x 1 byte (INT8) = 3 x 1600 x 64 x 12 = 3.7 MB. Attention scores (N x N x 12 heads): 1600 x 1600 x 12 x 1 byte = 30.7 MB. Total read+write: ~70 MB. | **4. Arithmetic Intensity:** AI = 7.8G / 70M = **~111 Ops/Byte.** | **5. Orin ridge point:** 275 TOPS / 204.8 GB/s = **1,342 Ops/Byte.** | **6. Diagnosis:** AI (111) << Ridge (1,342) => **memory-bandwidth bound.** The N x N attention matrix (30.7 MB) dominates memory traffic. | **7. Attainable throughput:** 204.8 GB/s x 111 = 22.7 TOPS (8.3% of peak). | **8. Mitigation:** Use Flash Attention to avoid materializing the N x N matrix, or reduce patch size to shrink N.

**Proposal A: Centralized A/B Test Pipeline** | Data collection: 10,000 vehicles x 500 MB video/month = 5 TB/month. Cellular upload at $0.10/GB: 5,000 GB x $0.10 = $500/month. Cloud compute (training + storage): $8,000/month. Data labeling: 10,000 vehicles x 10 clips x $3/clip = $300,000 (one-time). Engineering (3 engineers x 2 months): $150,000. **Year 1 total: ~$552,000.** | **Proposal B: Federated Learning** | FL platform engineering (5 engineers x 6 months): $750,000. FL server infra: $5,000/month x 12 = $60,000. Model updates: 1,000 vehicles/round x 50 MB x 52 weeks = 2,600 GB. Cellular at $10/GB: $26,000. Security audit + privacy review: $100,000. **Year 1 total: ~$936,000.** | **Verdict:** FL is 70% more expensive in Year 1 due to engineering CapEx. However, Year 2+ FL cost drops to ~$86,000/yr (no engineering), while centralized stays at ~$400K+/yr with ongoing labeling. FL breaks even in ~18 months.
fixes.realistic_solution: The correct analysis shows that the dominant factor in Year 1 TCO is the upfront engineering investment. A Federated Learning system requires building secure aggregation, differential privacy, device selection, and fault-tolerant client code — roughly 5 engineer-months more than a standard telemetry pipeline. In Year 1, FL costs ~$936K vs ~$552K for centralized. However, FL's costs are front-loaded: Year 2+ drops to ~$86K/yr because there is no recurring labeling cost. The centralized approach carries $300K+/yr in ongoing labeling and data costs. FL breaks even around month 18 and is the clear winner for any multi-year deployment.

fixes.napkin_math: **1. Calculate implied bandwidth:** BW = Data / Time = 512 MB / 15 ms = 512 / 0.015 = **~34.1 GB/s.** | **2. Compare to known interconnect specs:** PCIe Gen3 x16: ~16 GB/s (bidirectional ~32 GB/s, but P2P is unidirectional). PCIe Gen4 x16: ~32 GB/s unidirectional. PCIe Gen5 x16: ~64 GB/s unidirectional. NVLink 3.0 (A100): ~600 GB/s. NVLink 4.0 (H100): ~900 GB/s. | **3. Diagnosis:** 34.1 GB/s matches **PCIe Gen4 x16** (31.5 GB/s theoretical, ~34 GB/s with overhead rounding suggests near-peak Gen4 or a Gen5 x8 link at ~32 GB/s). This is NOT NVLink (which would transfer 512 MB in <1 ms). The system uses PCIe peer-to-peer (GPUDirect P2P), likely Gen4 x16. | **4. Fix options:** (a) Upgrade to NVLink-capable GPUs (~40x faster transfer, <0.4 ms). (b) Overlap compute and transfer by pipelining LiDAR processing — start transferring partial results while GPU B is still computing. (c) Reduce tensor size via compression or lower precision (FP16 -> INT8 halves transfer to ~7.5 ms).
fixes.realistic_solution: The bottleneck is the interconnect between the GPUs. A 15 ms transfer time for 512 MB implies ~34 GB/s bandwidth, which is consistent with a PCIe Gen4 x16 link (or PCIe Gen5 x8). NVLink would complete this transfer in under 1 ms, so its absence confirms a PCIe-based topology — typical in edge/automotive compute boxes that use consumer or embedded GPU configurations rather than server-grade NVLink bridges. The fix is either to upgrade the interconnect, overlap communication with computation via pipelining, or reduce the tensor transfer size.

fixes.napkin_math: **Scenario 1: Centralized with Anonymization** | Data per vehicle: 200 MB/month anonymized telemetry. Fleet upload: 100,000 x 200 MB = 20 TB/month. Cellular at $0.10/GB: 20,000 GB x $0.10 = $2,000/month. Cloud storage + compute: $8,000/month. Anonymization pipeline: $3,000/month. **Monthly total: $13,000. Annual: $156,000.** | **Scenario 2: Federated Learning** | Model update per vehicle: 5 MB/round. Participation: 1% (1,000 vehicles/round). Rounds/month: 8. Monthly data: 1,000 x 5 MB x 8 = 40 GB. Cellular at $10/GB: $400/month. FL server: $5,000/month. Engineering amortized: $20,000/month (first year). **Monthly total: $25,400. Annual: $304,800.** | **Comparison:** Centralized is $156K/yr; FL is $305K/yr (2x more in Year 1). **But:** FL Year 2+ drops to $65K/yr (no engineering amortization). A single privacy breach under GDPR ($20M+ fine) makes FL the risk-adjusted winner. The $149K/yr cost difference is insurance against catastrophic regulatory risk.

**Given:** Orin NX: 100 TOPS INT8, 102.4 GB/s LPDDR5. YOLOv8-M: ~39 GFLOPs, ~195 MB memory traffic per inference (weights ~26 MB + activations ~169 MB for 640x640 input). | **1. Arithmetic Intensity:** AI = 39G / 195M = **~200 Ops/Byte.** | **2. Ridge Point:** 100 TOPS / 102.4 GB/s = **~976 Ops/Byte.** | **3. Since AI (200) < Ridge (976), the model is memory-bandwidth bound.** | **4. Attainable throughput:** 102.4 GB/s x 200 Ops/Byte = **20.5 TOPS** (20.5% of peak). | **5. Per-frame compute time:** 39 GFLOPs / 20.5 TOPS = **1.9 ms compute.** | **6. Per-frame memory time:** 195 MB / 102.4 GB/s = **1.9 ms** (confirms BW-bound). | **7. With 70% BW utilization:** Effective BW = 0.7 x 102.4 = 71.7 GB/s. Memory time = 195 / 71.7 = **2.7 ms/frame.** | **8. But YOLOv8 has many layers with low reuse:** strided convolutions and activation spills cause repeated DRAM fetches, inflating actual memory traffic ~25x to ~4.8 GB/frame. Actual time: 4.8 GB / 71.7 GB/s = **67 ms/frame => ~15 FPS.** | **Conclusion:** The profiler shows 70% BW utilization, NOT compute utilization. The GPU's INT8 cores sit mostly idle. Fix: layer fusion to keep activations in SRAM, reduce input resolution, or use depth-wise separable convolutions.
fixes.realistic_solution: The profiler's 70% figure is memory bandwidth utilization, not compute utilization. The Orin NX has 102.4 GB/s LPDDR5 bandwidth and a ridge point of ~976 Ops/Byte (100 TOPS / 102.4 GB/s). YOLOv8-M has a nominal arithmetic intensity of ~200 Ops/Byte — well below the ridge point. The model is memory-bandwidth bound: the INT8 cores are starved waiting for data. However, the naive calculation suggests ~2 ms per frame, which contradicts the observed 15 FPS (67 ms). The gap is explained by activation spills: YOLOv8's many layers with low temporal reuse cause activations to be evicted from on-chip SRAM and re-fetched from DRAM, inflating effective memory traffic by ~25x. At 70% BW utilization, the real bottleneck is this amplified DRAM traffic. The fix is layer fusion to keep activations on-chip, not buying a faster accelerator.

fixes.napkin_math: **System Constraints:** Compute per accelerator: 26 TOPS (26e12 Ops/sec). Interconnect: PCIe Gen4 x8 = 16 GB/s. Vision Tower: 10 GFLOPs each. Fusion Block: 5 GFLOPs. Latency Budget: 33 ms. | **Option 1: Pipeline Parallelism** | Phase 1: Both chips run their vision towers in parallel. Time = 10G / 26T = **0.38 ms.** Phase 2: Transfer one vision output to the fusion chip. Assume vision output tensor ~4 MB. Transfer = 4 MB / 16 GB/s = **0.25 ms.** Phase 3: Fusion block on one chip: 5G / 26T = **0.19 ms.** **Total: 0.38 + 0.25 + 0.19 = 0.82 ms. Meets 33 ms budget with 40x margin.** Communication: 1 transfer of 4 MB. | **Option 2: Tensor Parallelism** | Each layer is split across both chips. Per-layer communication: 2 AllReduce ops (one per tensor half). Assume ~50 layers, each needing 0.5 MB sync. Transfer per sync: 0.5 MB / 16 GB/s = 0.03 ms. Total comm: 50 x 2 x 0.03 = **3.0 ms.** Compute per chip: 12.5G / 26T = 0.48 ms. **Total: 0.48 + 3.0 = 3.48 ms. Meets budget but 4x more communication.** | **Verdict:** Pipeline parallelism is the clear winner. One 4 MB transfer vs 100 sync events. On a 16 GB/s PCIe link, TP's frequent small transfers add up. Pipeline's single bulk transfer is far more efficient for edge interconnects.

**System:** 20B param ViT (FP16 = 40 GB), 2x Orin (275 TOPS INT8 each, ~17.5 TFLOPS FP16), 40 Gbps = 5 GB/s link, 33 ms deadline. | **Tensor Parallelism (TP):** Each transformer layer requires 2 AllReduce ops. Activation tensor per layer: ~20 MB (batch=1, seq=1600, hidden=6400, FP16). 80 layers x 2 x 20 MB = 3,200 MB total communication. Transfer time: 3,200 MB / 5 GB/s = **640 ms.** Far exceeds 33 ms budget. **TP is infeasible.** | **Pipeline Parallelism (PP):** Split model at layer 40. One activation tensor transfer per micro-batch: 20 MB. Transfer time: 20 MB / 5 GB/s = **4 ms.** Compute per half (40 layers on each Orin): 40/80 x total_FLOPs. Estimated ~200 GFLOPs per half at FP16. Time: 200G / 17.5T = **11.4 ms.** **Total: 11.4 + 4 + 11.4 = 26.8 ms. Meets 33 ms deadline.** | **Verdict:** PP requires 1 transfer (20 MB, 4 ms). TP requires 160 transfers (3.2 GB, 640 ms). On a 5 GB/s edge link, TP is 30x over budget. PP is the only viable strategy.
fixes.realistic_solution: Pipeline Parallelism is the only viable strategy. The critical insight is that Tensor Parallelism requires AllReduce communication at every transformer layer — with 80 layers and a 5 GB/s automotive ethernet link, the communication overhead alone is ~640 ms, which is 19x over the 33 ms budget. Pipeline Parallelism requires only a single activation transfer between the two halves of the model (~20 MB, taking 4 ms), keeping total latency under 27 ms. The 40 Gbps link is fast enough for one bulk transfer per frame but far too slow for the hundreds of synchronization events TP demands.

fixes.napkin_math: **System:** 2B param ViT (FP16 = 4 GB model), 4x Edge SoCs, 50 GB/s all-to-all, 33 ms deadline. | **Data Parallelism (gradient sync):** Gradient size = model size = 4 GB. AllReduce with ring algorithm: 2 x (N-1)/N x 4 GB = 2 x 0.75 x 4 = 6 GB data moved. Time: 6 GB / 50 GB/s = **120 ms.** Exceeds 33 ms. **DP is infeasible for real-time inference** (and DP is for training, not inference anyway). | **Tensor Parallelism:** 2B params / ~100 layers. Each layer needs 2 AllReduce on activations (~2 MB each). Total: 100 x 2 x 2 MB = 400 MB. Time: 400 MB / 50 GB/s = **8 ms.** Compute: 2B params x 2 FLOPs/param = 4 GFLOPs. Per SoC (assume 50 TOPS INT8): compute time negligible. **Total: ~8 ms compute + 8 ms comm = ~16 ms. Meets budget.** | **Pipeline Parallelism:** 4 stages, 25 layers each. Single activation transfer: 2 MB, 3 transfers. Time: 3 x 2 MB / 50 GB/s = 0.12 ms. Compute per stage: 1 GFLOPs. **Total: ~4 ms + 0.12 ms = ~4.1 ms. Meets budget with large margin.** | **Pipeline bubble overhead:** With batch=1, bubble ratio = (N-1)/N = 75%. Effective utilization only 25%. But absolute latency still wins. | **Verdict:** PP has lowest latency (~4 ms) but wastes 75% of compute. TP has higher latency (~16 ms) but uses all SoCs simultaneously. Both meet 33 ms. PP is simpler; TP is better if you need the compute headroom for larger models.

**Given:** GPU: 275 TOPS INT8, ~30W TDP, ~8W idle. DLA: 40 TOPS INT8, ~5W TDP, ~0.5W idle. EfficientNet-B0: 0.4 GFLOPs INT8. | **1. GPU inference time:** Utilization = 0.4G / 275T = 0.0015 (0.15%). Time ≈ 0.4G / 275T = **0.0015 ms** (negligible). But GPU has minimum dispatch overhead of ~1 ms. Effective: **~1 ms.** | **2. GPU energy per inference:** GPU power at 0.15% utilization ≈ idle power = ~8W (cores barely activate). Energy = 8W x 1 ms = **8 mJ.** | **3. DLA inference time:** Utilization = 0.4G / 40T = 1%. Time ≈ **0.01 ms**, but with overhead ~0.5 ms effective. | **4. DLA energy per inference:** DLA active power at 1% util ≈ ~1.5W. Energy = 1.5W x 0.5 ms = **0.75 mJ.** | **5. Average power at 10 FPS:** GPU: 8W idle + negligible compute = **~8W continuous.** DLA: 0.5W idle + 10 x 0.75 mJ/frame = 0.5W + 0.0075W = **~0.5W continuous.** | **6. Battery life at 10 FPS:** 50 Wh battery. GPU: 50 / 8 = **6.25 hours.** DLA: 50 / 0.5 = **100 hours.** | **7. Rule of thumb:** If model uses <5% of GPU peak TOPS, the DLA is almost always more energy-efficient because the GPU cannot clock-gate its massive core array effectively for tiny workloads. DLA is purpose-built for this regime.
fixes.realistic_solution: Compare energy, not just latency. The GPU has 275 TOPS but EfficientNet-B0 uses only 0.15% of that — the GPU cannot clock-gate effectively and draws ~8W idle power regardless. The DLA at 40 TOPS runs the same model at slightly higher utilization and draws only ~1.5W active / 0.5W idle. Energy per inference: GPU ≈ 8 mJ, DLA ≈ 0.75 mJ (10x more efficient). Average power at 10 FPS: GPU ~8W, DLA ~0.5W (16x difference). On a 50 Wh battery, GPU lasts ~6 hours while DLA lasts ~100 hours. The junior engineer is wrong — raw TOPS is irrelevant when the workload is 1000x smaller than peak capacity. The DLA is the correct choice for battery-powered deployment.

fixes.napkin_math: 1. **Convert file size to bits:** The container is 8 GigaBytes (GB). 8 GB x 8 bits/Byte = 64 Gigabits (Gb). | 2. **Convert bandwidth to matching units:** 100 Megabits/sec = 0.1 Gigabits/sec. | 3. **Calculate download time:** Time = Data / Bandwidth = 64 Gb / 0.1 Gb/s = **640 seconds ≈ 10.7 minutes.** | 4. **Sanity check:** At 100 Mbps, you download 12.5 MB/s. For 8 GB: 8,000 MB / 12.5 MB/s = 640 seconds. Confirmed. | 5. **Fleet consideration:** All vehicles download simultaneously, but each uses its own cellular connection. The bottleneck is per-vehicle bandwidth, not aggregate. However, if using a CDN, peak demand of 100K vehicles x 100 Mbps = 10 Tbps of CDN egress.

**Naive approach:** 20,000 devices x 50 metric series x 4 samples/min = 4,000,000 samples/min = **66,667 samples/sec.** With 10 histogram buckets per series: 20,000 x 50 x 10 = 10M active time series (Prometheus struggles above ~1M). Data volume: 4,000,000 samples/min x 60 min x 24 hr x 16 bytes/sample = **92 GB/day.** Cloud storage: 92 GB x $0.023/GB = $2.12/day. Cloud compute for Prometheus: ~$2,000/month (beefy instance for 10M series). | **Edge-aggregated approach:** Each device computes local aggregates (p50, p99, mean, count) per 5-min window. Upload: 20,000 devices x 10 aggregate metrics x 12 uploads/hr x 24 hr x 100 bytes = **576 MB/day.** Active series: 20,000 x 10 = 200K (well within Prometheus limits). | **Reduction:** 92 GB -> 576 MB = **~160x reduction** in data volume. Cloud compute: ~$100/month (small instance handles 200K series easily). | **Cost savings:** From ~$2,000/month to ~$100/month in compute, plus ~$60/month -> ~$0.40/month in storage.

**Single model:** Adversarial patch success rate = 95%. Miss rate (correct detection) = 5%. | **Dual independent models (defense):** If the two models are truly independent with identical 95% attack success, the probability BOTH are fooled = 0.95 x 0.95 = **90.25%.** This is NOT sufficient defense. However, if models use different architectures (e.g., CNN + ViT) with uncorrelated failure modes, empirically the joint attack success drops to ~20-40% because adversarial patches are architecture-specific. | **Map prior + model ensemble:** Attacker must fool the model AND be at a location where the map database expects a speed limit sign (not a stop sign). If map coverage is 99% accurate: attack success = 0.90 x 0.01 = **0.9%.** | **Temporal filter (majority vote over 30 frames):** Patch must cause misclassification in >15 of 30 frames. Physical patches work only at specific angles/distances. If per-frame success = 60% (due to angle variation): P(>15 of 30) = Σ(binomial) ≈ **3.5%.** | **Combined (ensemble + map + temporal):** << 0.1%. Defense in depth makes the attack economically infeasible.
fixes.realistic_solution: Defense in depth — no single layer is sufficient. A single adversarial patch defeats a single model with 95% confidence. Adding a second independent model does NOT reduce this to 5% — if both models have the same vulnerability, joint success is 0.95 x 0.95 = 90%. The key is architectural diversity: a CNN and a ViT fail on different adversarial patterns, reducing joint success to ~20-40%. Layer additional defenses: (1) HD map prior rejects 'speed limit sign' classifications at known stop sign locations, (2) temporal majority voting over 30 frames exploits the fact that physical patches only work at specific viewing angles, and (3) LiDAR/radar cross-modal verification provides a non-visual check. The combination makes successful attacks practically infeasible.

fixes.napkin_math: **Standard MDE formula (two-sample proportion test):** MDE = Z x √(2p(1-p)/n), where Z = Z_{α/2} + Z_β ≈ 2.8 (for α=0.05, power=80%). With p=0.5 (maximum variance): | **Naive independent (12 experiments, 2M DAU):** n per experiment = 2M / 12 = 167K. n per variant (A/B) = 83.5K. MDE = 2.8 x √(2 x 0.25 / 83,500) = 2.8 x √(0.00000599) = 2.8 x 0.00245 = **0.69%.** | **Layered (3 layers, 4 experiments each):** Each layer uses full 2M DAU. n per experiment = 2M / 4 = 500K. n per variant = 250K. MDE = 2.8 x √(2 x 0.25 / 250,000) = 2.8 x √(0.000002) = 2.8 x 0.00141 = **0.40%.** | **Improvement:** Layered design reduces MDE from 0.69% to 0.40% (1.7x more sensitive) while running the same number of experiments. | **Thermal throttling:** ~15% of budget Android devices throttle during daytime. Excluding them loses 15% sample (MDE increases ~8%) but eliminates the hardware confound causing contradictory results.

**LoRA adapter sizing:** Rank r=4, applied to Q and V projections. CLIP vision encoder hidden dim = 768, 12 layers. Trainable params per layer: 2 x (768 x 4 + 4 x 768) = 2 x 6,144 = 12,288. Total: 12 x 12,288 = **147,456 params (~148K).** Adapter size: 148K x 2 bytes (FP16) = **296 KB.** | **Training memory:** Frozen model: 300 MB (resident). LoRA params: 0.3 MB. Optimizer state (Adam: 2 momentum buffers): 2 x 0.3 = 0.6 MB. Activations (batch=1, 12 layers, ~768 x 197 patches x 2 bytes): ~3.6 MB/layer x 12 = 43 MB. **Total: ~344 MB.** | **Training time:** 200 images, 5 epochs, batch=1 = 1,000 forward+backward passes. FLOPs per pass: ~600 MFLOPs (LoRA backward through frozen forward). On mobile NPU at ~5 TOPS: 600M / 5T = 0.12 ms/pass. 1,000 x 0.12 = 0.12s compute. With memory overhead: **~30-60 seconds total.** | **Battery:** ~2W x 60s = 120 J. Phone battery ~15 Wh = 54,000 J. **Training cost: 0.22% of full charge.** | **Migration:** Back up 296 KB adapter to cloud. Restore on new phone. | **Annual energy:** 52 weeks x 120 J = 6,240 J = 6.24 kJ. Annual budget: 10 Wh/day x 365 = 3,650 Wh = 13.14 MJ. **Annual cost: 6.24 kJ / 13.14 MJ = 0.048%.**
fixes.realistic_solution: Design the full on-device training pipeline with realistic resource accounting: (1) LoRA adapters at rank 4 on Q/V projections yield ~148K trainable params (0.1% of 150M) in 296 KB — trivially small to store and migrate. (2) Training memory is ~344 MB — the frozen 300 MB model dominates, with activations adding ~43 MB. This fits within a modern phone's 6-8 GB RAM budget but requires careful memory management. (3) Training 200 images for 5 epochs takes ~30-60 seconds and costs ~0.22% of battery. (4) Phone migration is a 296 KB cloud backup — effectively instant. (5) Annual training energy is <0.05% of the phone's total energy budget.

fixes.napkin_math: **Memorization capacity analysis:** LSTM with 20M params at FP32 = 80 MB of learned weights. Information-theoretic capacity: ~20M x 32 bits = 640M bits. Average English word ≈ 5 chars x 8 bits = 40 bits. Theoretical max memorization: 640M / 40 = **16M tokens** (upper bound, assumes perfect encoding). | **Practical memorization:** Models don't memorize at theoretical capacity. With generalization overhead, empirically ~1-5% of capacity stores verbatim sequences. Practical verbatim memorization: ~160K-800K tokens. | **User data exposure:** User types ~10K words/day x 90 days = 900K tokens. If model memorizes ~500K tokens verbatim (mid estimate), that's **~56% of recent typing history potentially recoverable.** | **Attack surface:** Top-5 predictions x 1,000 queries = 5,000 probability observations. Each observation leaks ~2-3 bits about training data. Total leakage: ~10,000-15,000 bits ≈ 250-375 words reconstructable from the query budget. | **With DP (ε=8):** Noise injection limits per-query leakage to <0.3 bits. 1,000 queries x 0.3 = 300 bits ≈ 7-8 words. **~30x reduction in leakage.** Perplexity cost: base model 45, personalized 32, personalized+DP 35 (3-point cost for 30x privacy gain).

**Standard DP-SGD noise scale:** σ = (C × √(2 ln(1.25/δ))) / ε, where C = clipping norm, applied per-sample. At ε=2, δ=10⁻⁸, C=1.0: √(2 ln(1.25/10⁻⁸)) = √(2 x 18.95) = √37.9 = 6.16. σ_per_sample = 1.0 x 6.16 / 2 = **3.08.** | **With aggregation over n clients per round:** Noise per aggregate = σ_per_sample / √n. At n=1,000: σ_agg = 3.08 / √1000 = 3.08 / 31.6 = **0.097.** At n=10,000: σ_agg = 3.08 / √10000 = 3.08 / 100 = **0.031.** | **Signal-to-noise improvement:** 10x more clients => √10 ≈ 3.16x less noise — same privacy guarantee. | **LoRA dimension reduction:** Full model: 85M params. LoRA rank-8 on 768-dim model: ~600K trainable params. Ratio: 85M / 600K ≈ **142x fewer params.** Gradient signal concentrates into 142x fewer dimensions, dramatically improving SNR without touching ε. | **Combined fix:** 10x clients + LoRA = √10 x 142 ≈ **450x better effective SNR** at the same ε=2. This recovers most of the 40% perplexity gap without relaxing privacy.

**1. FP16 throughput:** Neural engine rated at 35 TOPS INT8. FP16 runs at half throughput: 35 / 2 = **17.5 TFLOPS FP16.** | **2. Inference time:** Model = 70 GFLOPs FP16. Time = 70G / 17.5T = **4 ms.** | **3. Jank budget:** 60 FPS => 1000 / 60 = **16.67 ms per frame.** | **4. Verdict:** 4 ms << 16.67 ms. The model uses only **24% of the frame budget,** leaving 12.67 ms for UI rendering, pre/post-processing, and display compositing. **No UI jank.** | **5. Utilization:** The model uses 70G / 17.5T = 0.004 seconds of the NPU's time per frame. At 60 FPS, total NPU usage = 0.004 x 60 = 0.24s per second of wall time = **24% NPU utilization.** Plenty of headroom for additional on-device ML tasks.
fixes.realistic_solution: The model will not cause UI jank. First, determine effective FP16 throughput: 35 TOPS INT8 / 2 = 17.5 TFLOPS FP16. Then compute inference time: 70 GFLOPs / 17.5 TFLOPS = 4 ms per frame. The 60 FPS target requires each frame to complete within 16.67 ms. At 4 ms, the model uses only 24% of the frame budget, leaving ample headroom for rendering and post-processing. The key insight is the 2x throughput penalty for FP16 vs INT8 — without this conversion, you would incorrectly estimate 2 ms and overstate the available headroom.

fixes.napkin_math: **1. Training frame size:** Resolution: 3840 x 2160 = 8,294,400 pixels. Bit depth: 16 bits (2 bytes) per pixel, RAW (single channel Bayer). Size = 8,294,400 x 2 = **16,588,800 bytes ≈ 16.6 MB.** | **2. Serving frame size:** Resolution: 224 x 224 = 50,176 pixels. Channels: 3 (RGB), 1 byte each. Size = 50,176 x 3 = **150,528 bytes ≈ 150 KB.** | **3. Data reduction factor:** 16,588,800 / 150,528 = **~110x reduction.** | **4. Breakdown of reduction:** Resolution: (3840 x 2160) / (224 x 224) = 8,294,400 / 50,176 = **165x** fewer pixels. Bit depth: 16-bit RAW → 8-bit RGB = **2x** fewer bytes per pixel. But RAW is 1 channel → RGB is 3 channels = **0.33x** (increases data). Net: 165 x 2 x 0.33 = **~110x.** | **5. Risk:** This 110x compression involves lossy transformations (demosaicing, color space conversion, aggressive downsampling) that the training pipeline never applied. The model learned features from 16-bit RAW at 4K — it will see different artifacts at 224px RGB.

**Given:** Single inference: 150 ms. Batch of 4: 300 ms total (75 ms/item amortized). | **No-Batching (Serial, 4 requests):** Each processed sequentially. First result at: 150 ms. Last result at: 4 x 150 = **600 ms.** Throughput: 4 / 600 ms = **6.67 edits/sec.** | **Batching (batch=4):** All 4 processed together. First result at: **300 ms** (must wait for entire batch). Last result at: **300 ms.** Throughput: 4 / 300 ms = **13.3 edits/sec.** | **The trade-off:** Throughput: Batching wins — 2x higher (13.3 vs 6.67 edits/sec). Latency to first result: Serial wins — 150 ms vs 300 ms (2x faster first response). | **For 3 requests (interpolating):** Serial: 3 x 150 = 450 ms. Batch-of-3 (estimated): overhead + compute ≈ 250-275 ms (sublinear scaling between 150 and 300). | **UX recommendation:** For interactive editing (user waiting for each result), serial is better — 150 ms feels instant. For bulk operations (apply filter to album), batching is better — 2x throughput. Hybrid: process first request immediately (150 ms), batch subsequent requests.
fixes.realistic_solution: This question tests the fundamental trade-off between latency and throughput. Serial processing gives the fastest time-to-first-result (150 ms), which matters for interactive UX. Batching gives 2x throughput (13.3 vs 6.67 edits/sec), which matters for bulk operations. The key insight: batching improves throughput by amortizing fixed NPU dispatch overhead, but it hurts perceived responsiveness because the user must wait for the entire batch. For a 'Magic Eraser' feature where the user makes one selection at a time, serial is correct — 150 ms is imperceptible. Only switch to batching if the user queues multiple edits (e.g., 'erase all selected objects').
